phase1_details.reason: All three involve standardizing/fixing inconsistent date formats in datasets. dana_285 is most specific (corporate treasury, ERP upload), dana_029 is archival date standardization, dana_122 is accepting a date format fix in public health — same core task of resolving inconsistent date formats.

All three involve tracking hours and billing/invoicing for client work. dana_224 (freelancer tracking project hours and invoices), dana_043 (consulting firm time tracking for client billing), and dana_231 (small law firm tracking case hours and billing) share the same domain and user goal.

Both involve tracking financial performance/ROI across clients or influencers and platforms. dana_248 (freelance illustrator tracking payments and effective earnings by client type) and dana_090 (influencer marketing ROI by influencer and platform) share the goal of analyzing revenue/return by category.

Both involve a language learner tracking flashcard reviews and analyzing retention/progress stats. dana_402 adds chart creation but the core scenario — flashcard review tracking and progress analysis — is identical.

Both involve converting/standardizing date/timestamp fields to proper typed formats for downstream use. dana_011 (compliance analyst ensuring date fields are properly typed) and dana_070 (server log analysis converting string timestamps to datetime) share the same core data-cleaning task.

Both involve analyzing sales patterns over time periods (seasonal/day-by-day) for a retail/event context. dana_250 (art fair booth sales and day-by-day trajectory) and dana_046 (Etsy seller analyzing seasonal sales patterns) share the same goal of identifying temporal sales trends.

Both involve preparing/updating a marketing performance report or chart for a client or presentation. dana_226 (marketing consultant preparing campaign performance report) and dana_099 (marketing analyst updating a chart for a board presentation) share the same domain and reporting goal.

Both involve filling in missing categorical values in rows by inferring from other data in the same dataset. dana_438 (NGO logistics: inferring missing destination region from city) and dana_442 (mural artist: filling missing paint brand names from previous entries) are the same fill-down/inference task in different domains.

Both involve combining data from multiple sources and then computing summary metrics. dana_095 (HR analyst combining headcount data from two systems) and dana_441 (city parks combining volunteer hours from three sign-in sheets then trending) share the same merge-then-summarize workflow.

Both involve comparing metrics year-over-year across organizational units to identify which are improving or declining. dana_055 (education department comparing graduation rates across districts YoY) and dana_235 (city budget analyst comparing departmental spending FY2022 vs FY2023) share the same comparative period-over-period analysis goal.

Both are about maintaining a plant care log and performing a bulk replace/edit operation on the dataset (with dana_017 focusing on undoing a mistaken replace). Same domain (gardening/plant logs) and same underlying goal of correcting/controlling edits to the log data.

All involve defining standardized/reusable business metrics (KPIs) in a semantic layer/data model so they can be consistently reused later (e.g., EBITDA margin, gross margin, portfolio return/drawdown formulas). Same goal: create reusable metric definitions for consistent reporting/analysis.
All scenarios involve analyzing SEO performance or keyword rankings, with similar goals of understanding traffic or ranking changes.
Both scenarios involve working with machine learning datasets, focusing on data preparation and cleaning.
Both scenarios involve analyzing marketing campaign data, with similar goals of understanding engagement or performance trends.
Both scenarios involve analyzing program outcomes or training completion data, with similar goals of measuring impact or performance.
Both scenarios involve analyzing customer or user data for marketing or optimization purposes.
Both scenarios involve analyzing marketing or referral program performance, with similar goals of understanding growth or sales impact.
Both scenarios involve cleaning or analyzing sensor data, with similar goals of addressing data quality issues.
Both scenarios involve reshaping data into a long format for easier analysis or filtering.
Both scenarios involve splitting combined columns into separate fields for easier filtering or reporting.
Both scenarios involve cleaning or validating datasets to ensure data quality before analysis.
Both scenarios involve analyzing ticket sales or donations to understand marketing or audience preferences.
Both scenarios involve standardizing or validating data before importing or reviewing it.
Both scenarios involve analyzing marketing or engagement data for reporting or segmentation purposes.
Both scenarios involve analyzing political or polling data, with similar goals of understanding trends or affiliations.
Both scenarios involve analyzing transaction or purchase data for personal or business insights.
Both scenarios involve cleaning or removing duplicate data from datasets before sharing or further analysis.
Both scenarios involve analyzing game or playtest data to improve user experience or balance.
Both scenarios involve tracking and analyzing personal hobby data, with similar goals of understanding trends or performance.
Both scenarios involve merging or preparing datasets for further analysis or distribution.
Both scenarios involve analyzing lab or scientific data, with similar goals of understanding results or quality.
Both involve carrying forward the last valid value to fill gaps in a time-series or log dataset (forward-fill / last-observation-carried-forward), same technique and goal.
Both involve a marketing/brand manager analyzing performance across social media platforms to inform strategy, same domain and user goal.
Both involve analyzing social media platform performance data and reporting engagement/growth trends, same core goal despite slightly different roles.
Both involve marketing analysts merging customer data sources to analyze customer value/LTV and channel performance, same domain and goal.
Both involve merging/joining CRM or customer data with another source to build customer profiles or churn analysis, same domain and analytical goal.
Both involve startup financial leadership (CFO/CEO) reviewing key financial metrics (burn rate, runway, investor metrics) for a startup, same domain and goal.
Both involve cleaning/standardizing inconsistent formatting (phone numbers, dates, IDs) in a dataset before uploading or publishing, same core data-cleaning goal.
Both involve validating categorical field values against an approved list before exporting/submitting a clean file, same validation workflow and goal.
Both involve validating coded/categorical values in a dataset against an approved reference list before sharing with a downstream stakeholder, same goal.
Both involve a data analyst reviewing an initial dataset profile and asking for suggested next analytical steps/breakdowns, same meta-analytical goal.
Both involve handling missing time points in scientific/sensor time-series data through interpolation or merging into a continuous dataset, same domain and goal.
Both are content marketers analyzing blog performance metrics to produce a report for prioritization/justifying budget (same domain: blog analytics; same goal: performance reporting).

Both involve redesigning an existing chart for a presentation/annual report deck with clearer labels and consistent colors (same domain: chart redesign for stakeholder deck; same goal: improve readability/branding).
Both are personal tracking charts that need visual restyling/cleanup for sharing (same domain: personal data visualization; same goal: restyle chart without changing underlying data).

Both are CRM data-cleaning scenarios focused on validating/standardizing fields before a downstream system/campaign use (same domain: CRM hygiene; same goal: clean/validate CRM export prior to activation/sync).
Both require combining two fields into a single standardized identifier/code for records (same domain: data wrangling/record standardization; same goal: create a composite reference/ID field).
Both scenarios involve cleaning HR data where duplicate entries need to be resolved, one for headcount reconciliation and the other for Workday export formatting.
Both scenarios involve preparing reports for executive review, one for workforce attrition and the other for key growth metrics.
Both scenarios involve standardizing inconsistent data entries across different systems or formats, one for OS versions and the other for product categories.
Both scenarios involve analyzing performance data across different locations or regions, one for retail sales and the other for coffee shop chains.
Both scenarios involve tracking and optimizing social media or engagement metrics, one for paid campaigns and the other for community management.
Both scenarios involve Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) tasks related to analyzing or reformatting technical data, one for API latency and the other for incident logs.
Both scenarios involve analyzing performance metrics, one for customer feedback and the other for blog content.
Both scenarios involve data engineering tasks, one for schema normalization and the other for network utilization auditing.
Both scenarios involve economic research, one for inflation metrics and the other for inflation-adjusted indicators.
Both scenarios involve preparing reports on performance metrics, one for abandoned cart recovery and the other for sell-through rate.
Both scenarios involve configuring preferences for consistent data handling, one for enterprise analytics and the other for personal budgeting.
Both involve analyzing lead conversion rates by source/stage and campaign — same domain (sales/lead generation) and same analytical goal.

Both describe datasets where identifying information (household/client fields) is only entered once per group and left blank for subsequent rows, requiring a fill-down operation for analysis — same problem and goal.
